title: Multi-Monitor and HiDPI Support: Building Resolution-Independent Linux Apps
subtitle: Master the complexities of DPI scaling and multi-monitor configurations in OpenMaui to create Linux desktop apps that look crisp on any display.
pullQuotes: Linux desktop environments present unique challenges: GNOME, KDE, and XFCE each implement different scaling policies, and users expect applications to respect their carefully tuned display configurations.

XRandR provides the foundational API for querying monitor information on X11, exposing physical dimensions, current resolution, refresh rates, and crucially, the position of each monitor in the virtual desktop space.
The key insight is that HiDPI support isn't just about rendering at higher resolution—it's about maintaining consistent physical sizes across different pixel densities.

sections.header: Introduction to HiDPI Challenges on Linux
sections.content:
Building resolution-independent applications on Linux presents unique challenges that don't exist on more homogeneous platforms. While macOS has standardized Retina displays and Windows has unified DPI scaling policies, Linux desktop environments present a fragmented landscape where users mix 4K displays with legacy 1080p monitors, run different desktop environments (GNOME, KDE, XFCE), and expect pixel-perfect rendering across all configurations.

OpenMaui addresses these challenges head-on by implementing comprehensive multi-monitor detection and per-monitor DPI scaling. The platform must handle:

- **Mixed DPI environments**: A user might have a 27" 4K monitor (163 DPI) alongside a 24" 1080p display (92 DPI)
- **Fractional scaling**: GNOME and KDE support 125%, 150%, 175%, and 200% scaling factors
- **Desktop environment differences**: Each DE exposes scaling settings through different mechanisms (GSettings, KConfig, XFCE configuration files)
- **Display server variations**: X11 and Wayland handle DPI differently, with Wayland delegating more responsibility to compositors

The key insight is that HiDPI support isn't just about rendering at higher resolution—it's about maintaining **consistent physical sizes** across different pixel densities. A button that measures 10mm × 5mm on a standard display should occupy the same physical space on a 4K display, even though it uses 4× the pixels.

OpenMaui's architecture leverages **SkiaSharp's vector-based rendering** to achieve true resolution independence. Unlike bitmap-based UI frameworks that scale rasterized images (leading to blur), Skia renders primitives at the target resolution, ensuring crisp text and graphics at any scale factor.
